Shant Khayalian — Balian’s Deep Tech Venture StudioKafka consumer lag is not the problem.
It is the symptom.
The real problem is usually hiding somewhere else.
slow processing
bad partitioning
database bottlenecks
wrong poll settings
consumer rebalances
downstream API latency
large messages
bad offset commits
bad retry strategyConsumer lag grows when your consumers cannot process records as fast as producers publish them. Kafka’s own monitoring docs recommend watching consumer side lag and message rates because a consumer can only keep up when lag stays below an acceptable threshold and fetch/consume activity continues.
In Spring Boot, the problem often hides inside one clean looking method:
@KafkaListener(topics = "orders", groupId = "billing-service")
public void consume(OrderCreated event) {
billingService.createInvoice(event);
}Looks simple.
But production asks harder questions:
How long does this method take?
Does it write to a database?
Does it call another API?
Does it retry forever?
Does it block the consumer thread?
Does it commit offsets too early?
Does it trigger rebalances?
Can it process faster than producers publish?
